title: The Post-Choice Society: Algorithmic Prediction and the Decentring of Choice
author: Schwarz

opening_move.description: The paper opens by noting that choice has been the defining social technology of late-modern Western capitalism — yet predictive algorithms are now systematically decentring it, replacing consumer choice with algorithmic prediction in social media, streaming, retail, and governance. This transformation demands historical contextualization and analysis of its wider implications.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The decentring of choice threatens not only neoliberal subjectivity but, as algorithmic prediction expands toward governance, democratic legitimacy itself.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper synthesizes Foucauldian governmentality theory with political economy of capitalism (Boltanski and Chiapello, Bauman) and cognitive science/economics (choice overload, friction) to develop an account of post-neoliberal individuation without choice.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Discourse analysis of platform design decisions, industry statements, and secondary literature on TikTok, Netflix, Spotify, Amazon, Google, and predictive shopping models. Historical analysis of the evolution of social media platforms (four-stage periodization).
data_sources: Secondary literature on TikTok, Instagram, Facebook, Netflix, Spotify, Google, Amazon
Iyengar and Lepper choice overload experiments
Bhandari and Bimo interview study of TikTok users
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Sociological-historical reconstruction of the transition from 'choice as engine' to 'choice as friction'; Foucauldian analysis of subjectification and governmentality.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper uses empirical materials (platform design evolution, social-psychological research on choice overload) supportively, to illustrate a conceptual-normative argument about the post-choice society. The argument could stand without the specific cases but the cases give it sociological grounding. The normative concern about democracy distinguishes this from a purely descriptive account.

case_type.case_name: TikTokization of social media (TikTok's 'For You' feed and its adoption by Instagram, Twitter/X, Facebook)

confidence_notes: The epistemic mode is normative_with_qualitative_empirical because the argument is primarily conceptual-normative (what is happening to choice in late capitalism and why it matters for democracy), but the platform analysis and choice overload research play a supportive, not merely illustrative, role. The case study of TikTok and TikTokization is the most developed case and functions as a generative-illustrative anchor for the broader periodization argument. The normative stake is medium rather than high — the conclusion raises democratic threats without prescribing solutions. The genealogical_reconstruction of the 'era of choice' and the transition to 'choice as friction' is a core argumentative move, not merely background.
